conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I need to sell my single unit of X. My production cost was 40 ZUP, so I must sell for at least 41 ZUP to make a profit. Since this is the first proposal, I will set a price slightly above my cost to leave room for negotiation but not so high that it scares off the buyer immediately. I'll propose 45 ZUP.</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Hello Player BLUE. I have one unit of X available for sale. Since it cost me 40 ZUP to produce, I am offering it to you for 45 ZUP. Let me know if you are interested or if you have a counter-offer.</message>

<other player message> Hello Player RED. 45 ZUP is within my budget, but I was hoping to get a slightly better deal. Since your cost was 40 ZUP, would you accept 42 ZUP? This is still a profit for you and a good deal for me. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 42 </other player proposed trade>

<reason> The buyer countered with 42 ZUP. This is 2 ZUP above my cost of 40, so it is a valid profit. However, my initial ask was 45. I have one more proposal left (limit is 3). Instead of accepting 42 immediately, I should try to meet in the middle to maximize my profit. I will propose 43 ZUP. This shows willingness to negotiate but keeps the price higher than their offer. If they reject this, I will likely have to accept 42 or close to it on the next turn, as I cannot make a 4th proposal.</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 43 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I appreciate the counter-offer of 42 ZUP. While that is a profit for me, I was hoping to get closer to my initial asking price. How about we meet in the middle at 43 ZUP? This gives you a better deal than my starting offer while ensuring a decent return for me. Let me know if this works for you.</message>

<other player message> I understand you want a better return, but 42 ZUP is my absolute best and final offer. It is still a profit for you (above your 40 ZUP cost) and a great deal for me. If you cannot accept 42, I will have to walk away or reconsider my budget. Let me know if 42 works. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 42 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> The opponent has explicitly stated that 42 ZUP is their absolute final offer and they will walk away if I do not accept. My cost is 40 ZUP, so 42 ZUP guarantees a 2 ZUP profit. While I have one proposal remaining (limit is 3), proposing a higher amount (e.g., 43 ZUP) carries a very high risk of rejection, resulting in a 0 ZUP payoff. The marginal gain of 1 ZUP is not worth the risk of losing the entire deal. Therefore, the optimal strategy is to accept the offer of 42 ZUP immediately to secure the profit. I will not use my remaining proposal slot.</reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I understand your position. Since 42 ZUP is your final offer and it still provides a profit for me above my production cost, I am willing to accept it. Let's finalize the trade: I give you X: 1, and you give me ZUP: 42.</message>
